does: This paper introduces the Device Context Protocol (DCP), a lightweight communication framework enabling large language models to safely control microcontrollers and IoT devices. The core contribution is a compact binary protocol with a typical frame size under 50 bytes, combining a 6-byte header with CBOR-encoded payloads and optional HMAC authentication. The protocol embeds safety mechanisms directly as primitives: capability scoping defines what operations are permitted, range and type checks validate parameters before execution, dry-run evaluation allows consequence-free testing, and units-as-types ensure dimensional correctness. A host-side Bridge component running on more capable hardware validates all LLM-generated commands, rejecting hallucinated or malformed calls before transmission to the device. Reference implementation on ESP32 achieves 27.6 KB flash and 0.6 KB RAM footprint, making it viable for commodity microcontrollers with severe memory constraints.
connects: This work extends the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, which enables LLMs to invoke external tools and services, by addressing its fundamental unsuitability for embedded systems. Prior work including IoT-MCP attempted to port MCP to edge gateways at 74 KB memory, but still excluded the smallest commodity microcontrollers and ignored the critical safety problem inherent in giving unreliable LLM-driven callers direct hardware control. DCP responds to the broader challenge of LLM reliability in physical-world interaction, building on established practices in embedded systems design such as protocol framing and capability-based security, while integrating emerging safety patterns like constraint checking and dry-run semantics. The work sits at the intersection of IoT device protocols, LLM orchestration architectures, and safety-critical embedded systems engineering.
enables: By making protocol-layer safety primitives standard, DCP enables a new class of applications where LLMs orchestrate diverse heterogeneous devices without requiring custom safety layers per device or trusting LLM outputs directly. The compact frame size and minimal firmware footprint create a pathway for retrofitting existing IoT devices with LLM orchestration capabilities. The manifest-based capability scoping opens possibilities for fine-grained permission models over LLM-device interactions, analogous to capability-based security in operating systems. Future work could extend DCP to support hierarchical device topologies, formal verification of manifest constraints, or integration with stronger LLM safeguards that leverage the protocol's declared capabilities for improved prompt filtering. The language-neutral conformance suite and open-source reference implementations provide infrastructure for ecosystem adoption and standardization across diverse embedded platforms.
